coredata.dc:title: Rapid thermal annealing of nickel-carbon nanowires for graphene nanoribbons formation
coredata.dc:creator: Tosic, D.
Markovic, Z.
Jovanovic, S.
Prekodravac, J.
Budimir, M.
Kepic, D.
Holclajtner-Antunovic, I.
Dramicanin, M.
Todorovic-Markovic, B.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we present a novel method for graphene nanoribbons formation using nickel nanowires as templates. Modification of electrochemical procedure for nickel nanowires synthesis was introduced by implementing graphene quantum dots as a carbon source. Thus, nickel nanowires with graphene quantum dots were prepared. These wires were exposed to rapid thermal annealing at high temperatures (500°C, 700°C and 800°C). During this process carbon atoms dissolve in metal and precipitate to the nickel surface forming nickel-carbon nanowires. Atomic force microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and Raman spectroscopy were characterization methods used for analysis of changes in nickel-carbon nanowires properties depending on the different annealing temperatures. After etching nickel with nitric acid, graphene nanoribbons with average diameters of 32nm and average lengths of 4μm were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanoribbons
Nickel nanowires
Template synthesis
Graphene quantum dots
Rapid thermal annealing
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs as precursors for GNRs formation
Optimal conditions for formation of carbon nanowires with nickel cores

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia (project number 172003). This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union’s Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research has been further co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences. Also, the authors would like to thank to the colleagues from Institut de Ciència de Materials de Barcelona (ICMAB-CSIS) for SEM measurements and dr Dusan Milivojevic from Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences for help with an annealing process.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of GNRs formation process.
a) AFM image of GQDs on the Si surface. Inset represents TEM image of single GQD; b) Raman spectrum of GQDs.
a) FTIR spectrum of GQDs; b) TGA curve of GQDs.
AFM images of Ni-C NWs annealed at different temperatures: a) before annealing (inset—AFM camera view of sample surface) and after annealing at b) 500°C, c) 700°C, d) 800°C.

a) FTIR spectra of Ni-C NWs: before (1) and after annealing at 500°C (2), 700°C (3) and 800°C (4); b) Normalized Raman spectra of Ni-C NWs: before (1) and after annealing at 500°C (2), 700°C (3) and 800°C (4).
SEM image of Ni-C nanowires annealed at 500°C. a) bundles of Ni-C nanowires; b) clear boundary of carbon coated (transparent gray, area 1) and nickel (white, area 2) part of a nanowire.
AFM image of GNR with diameter of 30nm.
Photoluminescent spectrum of graphene nanoribbons. At the excitation wavelength of 375nm, GNRs emit blue light at 446nm.
EDS analysis of Ni-C nanowires after etching process at two different positions on the same wire (Fig. 5b).

head.ce:title: Rapid thermal annealing of nickel-carbon nanowires for graphene nanoribbons formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, University of Belgrade, Bulevar Kralja Aleksandra 73, 1100 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear Sciences Vinca, University of Belgrade, Mike Petrovica Alasa 12-14, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Institute, Slovak Academy of Sciences, Dubravska cesta 9, 84541 Bratislava, Slovakia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physical Chemistry, University of Belgrade, Studentski trg 12-16, 11158 Belgrade 118, POB 47, Serbia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Laboratory for Radiation Chemistry and Physics “Gamma”, Institute of Nuclear Sciences Vinca, University of Belgrade, Mike Petrovica Alasa 12-14, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we present a novel method for graphene nanoribbons formation using nickel nanowires as templates. Modification of electrochemical procedure for nickel nanowires synthesis was introduced by implementing graphene quantum dots as a carbon source. Thus, nickel nanowires with graphene quantum dots were prepared. These wires were exposed to rapid thermal annealing at high temperatures (500°C, 700°C and 800°C). During this process carbon atoms dissolve in metal and precipitate to the nickel surface forming nickel-carbon nanowires. Atomic force microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and Raman spectroscopy were characterization methods used for analysis of changes in nickel-carbon nanowires properties depending on the different annealing temperatures. After etching nickel with nitric acid, graphene nanoribbons with average diameters of 32nm and average lengths of 4μm were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a monolayer of sp2—hybridized carbon atoms organized in honeycomb lattice, is a semi metal (zero gap semiconductor) [1]. Graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), as quasi one dimensional graphene nanostructures, represent thin strips of graphene whose widths are of the order of nanometers. Due to the quantum confinement effect, they exhibit unique electrical properties different from two-dimensional graphene sheet [2]. Finite termination of graphene sheet results in opening a band gap in graphene nanoribbon structures, that is inversely proportional to the width [3]. Depending on the edge configuration (zigzag or armchair) and width, graphene nanoribbons show different electronic properties [4]. However, it has been experimentally shown that all GNRs with widths less than 10nm, show semiconducting behavior, regardless of their edge structure and can be used as room-temperature field effect transistors [5].

Different methods for GNRs synthesis have been developed, including chemical [6–8], sonochemical [3,5,9], lithographic [10–14] methods and the unzipping of carbon nanotubes [15–19]. In previous publications, we had shown a unique method which we have developed for GNRs synthesis by gamma irradiation of graphene [20]. Although it is a simple procedure, its disadvantage is a very high cost of production. Accordingly, we have continued to explore new ways of GNRs fabrication and developed a more cost-effective approach.

In this work, we present a novel template based method for graphene nanoribbons synthesis. Alumina membranes were used as templates, as pore diameter in membrane predetermines diameter of deposited nanowires and membrane itself can be easily removed. Well-known electrochemical procedure for synthesis of nickel nanowires [21] was modified by adding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which will be implemented in nickel nanowires and serve as a carbon source. GQDs represent 0D nanomaterial that consists of 10 graphene sheets, or less, with lateral size below 100nm [22]. Carbon nanomaterials, graphene and carbon nanotubes, are grown from different solid carbon sources such as polymer films or small molecules [23]. For this research, original method for application of graphene quantum dots as a carbon source was developed. GQDs were chosen as an ideal precursor material for GNR formation because of their good stability and solubility in a nickel bath [24]. After the electrodeposition and formation of nickel-carbon nanowires (Ni-C NWs), rapid thermal annealing process led to formation of thin uniform graphene layer on the surface of nickel nanowires. Nickel core was etched with nitric acid (HNO3) and graphene nanoribbons were obtained. Average diameter of GNRs was 32nm, with heights of 3nm.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized using a modified alkali-assisted electrochemical procedure [25]. Firstly, spectroscopic graphitic electrodes (Ringsdorff Werke GmbH, Bonn, Germany, 99.999% purity), with 3.05mm in diameter, were rinsed with MiliQ water and ethanol (98%) and used both as anode and cathode. The current intensity was set to 20mA. NaOH/EtOH solution in concentration 3% was used as electrolyte. After that, ethanol solution of GQDs was neutralized with hydrogen chloride (HCl), filtrated in order to remove sodium chloride (NaCl) and then evaporated to obtain GQD powder.

The alumina membranes (Whatman, Inc), with 0.2μm pore diameter, were used in the process of nickel electroplating as templates for obtaining Ni-C nanowires. One side of alumina membrane was painted with gallium indium (GaIn) eutectic alloy (75.5% Ga—24.5% In), which forms a conductive layer that serves as the cathode, while nickel wire served as the anode. Watts nickel platting solution (0.4M (NiCl2·6H2O); 0.28M (H3BO3); 0.32M (NiSO4·6H2O)) was used as an electrolyte in the process of electrodeposition. GQD powder, in concentration of 0.2g/l, was added to Watts solution and used as a carbon source. GQDs have carboxyl groups which are base condition deprotonated (COO− groups) and during the process of electrodeposition GQDs move with all other negative ions towards the cathode. Electrical circuit was established by KEITHLEY 220 programmable current source. Electrodeposition of nickel and GQDs in the pores of alumina membrane was carried out during 15min. After the process of electrodeposition, GaIn alloy was removed with concentrated nitric acid. Ni-C nanowires were freed from the filter by dissolving alumina in 6M sodium hydroxide and afterwards suspended in water.

Ten microliters of this suspension were deposited on freshly cleaned Si substrates. Silicon substrates were then put on a magnetic stirrer to manipulate wires so they are aligned with the magnetic field, and held for 5min at the temperature of 100°C to evaporate water. Samples were then annealed in the process of rapid thermal annealing at different temperatures (500°C, 700°C, 800°C) in vacuum for 30min, followed by rapid cooling in order to segregate carbon on nickel surface. After that, nickel was etched with nitric acid (HNO3) and graphene nanoribbons on Si substrates were obtained.

In order to etch nickel, first the bubbling transfer process was used to detach nanowires from the silicon substrate [26]. 10μl of polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was deposited over the sample and dried at 160°C during 5min. Then, in the process of electrolysis PMMA/nanowires layer was detached from the silicon substrate. In the electrolysis procedure the solution of NaOH was used as an electrolyte, while PMMA/nanowires/silicon served as the cathode and nickel wire as the anode. After rinsing it with MiliQ water, floating PMMA/nanowires layer was captured and immersed in nickel etching solution (HCl:HNO3 =4:1) for 5min. Finally, the PMMA was removed by acetone vapor.

Morphological features of Ni-C NWs and GNRs were investigated by Quesant/Ambios atomic force microscopy (AFM) system. Premounted standard silicon probes Q-WM300 (purchased from Nano and more) with a force constant of 40N/m and resonant frequency of 300kHz were used. All images were recorded in AFM tapping mode, in the air and at the room temperature conditions. For AFM image analysis, Gwyddion software was used [27]. Tip convolution artifact (a convolution of the shape of the probe, and the shape of the sample) is always present on AFM images. Accurate values for the diameters of particles were calculated from equation for the larger dimension of particles: rc =r (cosθ0 +(cos2θ0 +(1+sinθ0)(−1+(tgθ0/cosθ0)+tg2θ0))1/2) [28]. Samples were deposited on Si substrates as described above.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) observations were carried out on TEM JEOL Jem 1210 Electron microscope operating at 120kV. Samples were prepared by drop casting of nanowire dispersion on carbon coated copper grid with 300mesh.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) recordings were carried out on a QUANTA FEI 200 FEG-ESEM instrument, at 10–30kV under high vacuum conditions.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were recorded on Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10 FT-IR instrument at the room temperature. Spectral resolution was 4cm−1.

Raman spectra were recorded on a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific) using 532nm excitation line from a diode pumped solid state laser. Laser power was 1mW. The Raman spectra were recorded with an 1800 lines/mm grating. The spot size of laser beam was 0.7mm. The spectral resolution was 0.5cm−1. Acquisition time for all spectra was 100s (10×10s).
Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out under nitrogen flow of 30ml/min using Mettler Toledo TGA/DSC STAR system at a ramp rate of 10°C/min.

The photoluminescence spectra of graphene nanoribbons on Si/SiO2 substrate were recorded at room temperature on a Fluorolog-3 Model FL 3-221 spectrofluorometer system (HORIBA Jobin-Yvon S.A.S., Chilly Mazarin, France). Emission spectra were measured utilizing a 450W Xenon lamp as an excitation source and R928P photomultiplier tube as a detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs as precursors for GNRs formation
ce:section.ce:para: Particular novelty of this work is a modification of nickel plating bath by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into the bath solution [29]. GQDs have hydrophobic spherical graphene base and hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups [30]. GQDs were used as a carbon source and represent basic material for subsequent formation of GNRs. We chose GQDs to be a building block of GNRs on the basis that these two materials share the same fundamental sp2-hybridized hexagonal lattice structure of graphene.

The morphology of GQDs was examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques (Fig. 1 a). Statistical analysis of AFM images reveals that average height of GQDs is 1.7nm with average diameter of 20.7nm. AFM images also reveal uniform surface morphology and spherical shape of GQDs.

Structural properties of GQDs were characterized using Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectra of carbon nanomaterials show two main peaks: G peak which appears around 1580cm−1 and D peak around 1360cm−1 [31]. In Fig. 1b Raman spectra of GQDs with characteristic peaks at 1590cm−1 and 1360cm−1 are presented. Presence of G bands proves graphene structure of GQDs, while high intensity of D band indicates that this structure is very disordered and defective. Defects in Raman spectrum stem from GQDs edges and oxygen-containing functional groups, which are located both at the edges of GQDs or in their graphene core.

Chemical properties of GQDs were analyzed by means of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). Peaks near 1572cm−1, 1418cm−1 and 2980cm−1 can be assigned to CC stretching of graphite. Peak at 1011cm−1 stems from CO bonds in carboxyl groups and peak at 1365cm−1 stems from symmetric stretching of carboxyl groups. Peak at 1045cm−1 originates from CO stretching vibrations of alkoxy groups. Bands at 2697cm−1 and 2781cm−1 are attributed to CH vibration of aromatic aldehyde. Two peaks at 2930cm−1 and 2980cm−1 stem from stretching vibrations of CH and CH2 groups. The broad band at 3423cm−1 is associated with the OH stretching vibrations. These results display good hydrophilic properties of GQDs.

Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to quantitatively investigate the amount of oxygen-containing functional groups in the structure of GQDs. TGA curve (Fig. 2b) shows weight loss in the temperature interval of 110–147°C (∼14.13%) that can be attributed to the decomposition of functional groups [32]. The weight loss from 200 to 600°C is 5.91% and this change can be assigned to thermal decomposition of hydroxyl functional groups. Further increase in temperature (from 600 to 800°C) caused additional weight loss of 3.10%, which can be result of the decomposition of graphene sheet in graphene quantum dots. Based on TGA analysis it can be concluded that the percentage of oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs, is responsible for their high solubility in water, is 21.04%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for formation of carbon nanowires with nickel cores
ce:section.ce:para: The first step of our experiment was Ni-C nanowires formation through nickel electroplating procedure [21], that was altered by adding GQDs into nickel platting solution. Suspension of Ni-C nanowires in water was deposited on Si substrates and subjected to rapid thermal annealing (RTA) at different temperatures in order to induce carbon dissolution into nickel and its precipitation on the nickel surface [33]. Influence of different annealing temperatures (500°C, 700°C, 800°C) on graphene layer formation on the nickel surface was investigated with atomic force microscopy, Raman spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared techniques.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is a powerful technique for investigating surface features (height, length and diameter) of nanoparticles. Although AFM camera view of sample surface (Fig. 3 a—inset) reveals parallel distribution of nanowires due to influence of magnetic field during the deposition process, it is obvious that nanowires tend to gather up in bundles as a result of magnetic interaction [34]. Reduction of this effect is possible by decreasing the diameter of alumina pores, and consequently the diameter of obtained nanowires [35]. AFM microscopy was used to investigate influence of annealing temperature on the diameters and heights of Ni-C NWs. After analyzing more than 50 AFM images, we concluded that the average diameters and heights of Ni-C NWs before and after annealing (at 500°C, 700°C and 800°C) remain unchanged (∼300nm). Due to the frequent clustering of nanowires in bundles it was not possible to measure heights with high accuracy. Values of the diameters of nanowires were obtained by taking into account the convolution effect between sample surface and AFM tip [28].

Based on AFM image analysis, it is apparent that different annealing temperatures have different effects on the surface morphology of Ni-C NWs (Fig. 3). Before annealing (Fig. 3a), nanowires had lengths of more than 20μm, straight edges and very smooth surface. After annealing at 500°C (Fig. 3b), nanowires retained similar morphology. When temperature was increased to 700 and 800°C, nanowires were rived into discontinuous chains (Fig. 3c,d) with lengths from 1 to 4.5μm [36]. Average surface roughness (Ra) of nanowires was determined using Gwyddion software for AFM image analysis by calculating mean average for more than 20 nanowires for each sample. Initial Ra parameter for nanowires before annealing was ∼17.5nm. Results show that initial Ra value decreased for the samples annealed at 500°C (∼12.7nm) and increased for the samples annealed at 700°C and 800°C (∼18.9nm and ∼19.3nm, respectively). These results imply that the optimal annealing temperature for the formation of long Ni-C NWs with smooth surface is 500°C.

FTIR analysis was used to examine differences in chemical morphology of Ni-C NWs before and after annealing. FTIR spectrum of Ni-C nanowires before annealing (Fig. 4 a, line 1) shows a broad band at 1523cm−1 that can be attributed to aromatic structures in the cores of GQDs [37] and confirms their incorporation in the structure of nanowires during electrodeposition. Second relevant band appears at 2005cm−1 and denotes the presence of carboxylic groups [20]. After the annealing process, FTIR spectra at 500°C, 700°C and 800°C show bands at 1389cm−1, 1405cm−1 and 1408cm−1, respectively, which stem from carboxyl groups. Bands at around 1560–1580cm−1 at all three annealed samples are associated with the CC stretching of graphite which indicates the presence of sp2-hybridized graphene-like structures [38]. In the spectrum after annealing at 800°C, a band at 1205cm−1 is usually attributed to C-OH stretching vibrations and a peak at 2021cm−1 is associated with the COOH groups. At all four spectra, bands at around 1073cm−1 and 2366cm−1 are due to working in atmospheric conditions (CO2) and bands at around 1116cm−1 and 2362cm−1 stem from the influence of Si surface.

FTIR analysis confirms formation of sp2-hybridized graphene structures with oxygen-containing functional groups after annealing. Also, after the annealing process, FTIR spectra show more distinct peaks that characterize exact functional groups bonded to graphene layer. Influence of edges and vacancy defect induces a red shift in frequencies which originates from hydroxyl bending motions [39].

Raman spectroscopy was used to confirm the presence of thin graphene layer formed on the nickel surface after annealing process. Fig. 4b depicts Raman spectra, normalized to G band intensity, of Ni nanowires before and after annealing at 500, 700 and 800°C. All spectra show characteristic G band which indicates existence of graphene layer on nickel wires at all annealing temperatures. Also, the upshift in G band frequency, from 1571cm−1 for the sample before annealing to 1595cm−1 for the sample annealed at 800°C, is observed. Appearance of this blue shift (∼24cm−1) is a consequence of p-doping of graphene sheet formed on the surface on nickel nanowire [31,40].

It is assumed that appearance of broad D peak at around 1346cm−1 for all four samples is caused by defects in a discontinuous structure of graphene layer. Appearance of D band in Raman spectra is due to effects of edges and structural defects in graphene on the surface of Ni nanowires [41]. D band can also be associated with presence of oxygen functional groups in the structure of graphene based materials, which were detected in FTIR spectrum after annealing.

In this mid-step of GNRs formation another interesting nanostructure is obtained. Ni-C nanowires possess combined magnetic properties of nickel nanowires and electrical properties of graphene that has promising future in spintronics applications [36].

Ni-C NWs, with nickel core and graphene outer layer, were formed by annealing at 500°C. The last step in our experiment is etching of nickel in order to obtain carbon layer in a shape of a nanoribbon on the Si surface. Schematic representation of GNRs formation process is depicted in Scheme 1 .

Scanning electron microscopy and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy were used to characterize efficiency of nickel etching process. SEM images reveal evidence of dominant carbon coating of Ni nanowires and a residue of unetched nickel cores after annealing process (Fig. 5 a). Distinct difference in white parts of wire which stems from nickel and transparent gray areas which represent carbon structure is noticeable (Fig. 5). Results of EDS analysis are summarized in Table 1 , confirming different weight percentages of nickel on the two parts of a wire from Fig. 5b.

As can be seen on Fig. 5, on some Ni-C NWs nickel core has been completely etched. These hollow tubular nanostructures collapse and form multilayer graphene nanoribbons. Morphology of GNRs was investigated using atomic force microscopy (Fig. 6 ). Based on AFM image analysis, average dimensions of GNRs were calculated: diameter 32nm, height 3nm and length 4μm. Comparing these results with AFM images presented in Fig. 3, we can conclude that heights are reduced from around 350nm for Ni-C NWs to 3nm for graphene nanoribbons.

The photoluminescence spectrum of GNRs shows blue emission centered at 446nm, after excitation at 375nm wavelength (Fig. 7 ). The origin of photoluminescence in GNRs could be assigned to oxygen functional groups, zigzag edges and the effect of quantum confinement of electrons [42]. Many carbon nanoparticles exhibit blue emission upon excitation [43–45]. GQDs, which were used as precursors for GNRs synthesis, show excitation-dependent PL behavior and peak at around 460nm for excitation at 375nm [46]. This blue shift from 460 to 446nm confirms difference in shape, size, edge configuration and defects of these two materials [47,48].

It has been shown in the literature that PL peaks in graphene oxide spectra change with reduction from broad peaks at 600nm towards narrower peaks at shorter wavelengths at around 400nm [49]. Reduced graphene oxide exhibits blue emission at 440nm [45]. Therefore, GNRs posses a structure similar to the one of reduced graphene oxide. This result corresponds to FTIR analysis, which confirms the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, and also to Raman spectra after annealing.

In this work, a novel approach for synthesis of GNRs is presented. Average dimensions of GNRs are: 32nm in diameters with heights of 3nm and lengths of 4μm. GNRs exhibit blue photoluminescence located at 446nm. In future research, described procedure will be employed to obtain GNRs with different widths and lengths. Compared to other GNRs synthesis methods, main and unique advantage of this procedure is that GNRs can be easily manipulated and oriented in magnetic field, which makes them a promising candidate for application in nanoelectronics. Moreover, desired dimensions of GNRs can be determined in advance by choosing alumina membrane with appropriate pore diameter. Proposed method is highly cost efficient and can be done in several hours.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia (project number 172003). This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union’s Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research has been further co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences. Also, the authors would like to thank to the colleagues from Institut de Ciència de Materials de Barcelona (ICMAB-CSIS) for SEM measurements and dr Dusan Milivojevic from Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences for help with an annealing process.
